name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ وَلَوْ بَسَطَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِعِبَادِهِۦ لَبَغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِن يُنَزِّلُ بِقَدَرٍ مَّا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (٢٧)
```

```english
Were Allah to expand the provision for [all] His servants, they would surely create havoc on the earth. But He sends down in a [precise] measure whatever He wishes. Indeed, He is all-aware, all-seeing about His servants. (27)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُنَزِّلُ ٱلْغَيْثَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا قَنَطُوا۟ وَيَنشُرُ رَحْمَتَهُۥ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْوَلِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (٢٨)
```

```english
It is He who sends down the rain after they have been despondent, and unfolds His mercy, and He is the Guardian, the All-laudable. (28)
```

```arabic
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ خَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَثَّ فِيهِمَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ جَمْعِهِمْ إِذَا يَشَآءُ قَدِيرٌ (٢٩)
```

```english
Among His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth and whatever creatures He has scattered in them, and He is able to gather them whenever He wishes. (29)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَصَـٰبَكُم مِّن مُّصِيبَةٍ فَبِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَن كَثِيرٍ (٣٠)
```

```english
Whatever affliction that may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense of yours]. (30)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (٣١)
```

```english
You cannot frustrate [Allah] on the earth, and you do not have besides Allah any guardian or helper. (31)
```

```arabic
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِ ٱلْجَوَارِ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ كَٱلْأَعْلَـٰمِ (٣٢)
```

```english
Among His signs are the ships [that run] on the sea [appearing] like landmarks. (32)
```

```arabic
إِن يَشَأْ يُسْكِنِ ٱلرِّيحَ فَيَظْلَلْنَ رَوَاكِدَ عَلَىٰ ظَهْرِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ (٣٣)
```

```english
If He wishes He stills the wind, whereat they remain standstill on its surface. There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant]. (33)
```

```arabic
أَوْ يُوبِقْهُنَّ بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ وَيَعْفُ عَن كَثِيرٍ (٣٤)
```

```english
Or He wrecks them because of what they have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]. (34)
```

```arabic
وَيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا مَا لَهُم مِّن مَّحِيصٍ (٣٥)
```

```english
Let those who dispute Our signs know that there is no escape for them. (35)
```

```arabic
فَمَآ أُوتِيتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَمَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ (٣٦)
```

```english
Whatever you have been given are the wares of the life of this world, but what is with Allah is better and more lasting for those who have faith and who put their trust in their Lord (36)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبَـٰٓئِرَ ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ وَإِذَا مَا غَضِبُوا۟ هُمْ يَغْفِرُونَ (٣٧)
```

```english
—those who avoid major sins and indecencies, and forgive when angered; (37)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِرَبِّهِمْ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَمْرُهُمْ شُورَىٰ بَيْنَهُمْ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (٣٨)
```

```english
those who answer their Lord, maintain the prayer, and [conduct] their affairs by counsel among themselves, and they spend out of what We have provided them; (38)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَآ أَصَابَهُمُ ٱلْبَغْىُ هُمْ يَنتَصِرُونَ (٣٩)
```

```english
those who, when afflicted by oppression, defend themselves. (39)
```

```arabic
وَجَزَٰٓؤُا۟ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِّثْلُهَا ۖ فَمَنْ عَفَا وَأَصْلَحَ فَأَجْرُهُۥ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٠)
```

```english
The requital of evil is an evil like it, so whoever excuses and conciliates, his reward lies with Allah. Indeed, He does not like the wrongdoers. (40)
```

```arabic
وَلَمَنِ ٱنتَصَرَ بَعْدَ ظُلْمِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَا عَلَيْهِم مِّن سَبِيلٍ (٤١)
```

```english
As for those who retaliate after being wronged, there is no ground for action against them. (41)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا ٱلسَّبِيلُ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَظْلِمُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ وَيَبْغُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٤٢)
```

```english
The ground for action is only against those who oppress the people and commit tyranny in the land in violation of justice. For such there will be a painful punishment. (42)
```

```arabic
وَلَمَن صَبَرَ وَغَفَرَ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ (٤٣)
```

```english
As for someone who endures patiently and forgives—that is indeed the steadiest of courses. (43)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن وَلِىٍّ مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ ۗ وَتَرَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ يَقُولُونَ هَلْ إِلَىٰ مَرَدٍّ مِّن سَبِيلٍ (٤٤)
```

```english
Those whom Allah leads astray have no guardian apart from Him. You will see the wrongdoers, when they sight the punishment, saying, ‘Is there any way for a retreat?’ (44)
```

```arabic
وَتَرَىٰهُمْ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا خَـٰشِعِينَ مِنَ ٱلذُّلِّ يَنظُرُونَ مِن طَرْفٍ خَفِىٍّ ۗ وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَأَهْلِيهِمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ فِى عَذَابٍ مُّقِيمٍ (٤٥)
```

```english
You will see them being exposed to it, humbled by abasement, furtively looking askance. The faithful will say, ‘Indeed the losers are those who have ruined themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed, the wrongdoers will abide in lasting punishment. (45)
```

```arabic
وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ يَنصُرُونَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن سَبِيلٍ (٤٦)
```

```english
They have no awliya to help them besides Allah. There is no way out for those whom Allah leads astray.’ (46)
```

```arabic
ٱسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لِرَبِّكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا مَرَدَّ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ مَا لَكُم مِّن مَّلْجَإٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّكِيرٍ (٤٧)
```

```english
Respond to your Lord before there comes a day for which there will be no revoking from Allah. On that day you will have no refuge, nor will you have [any chance for] denial [of your sins]. (47)
```

```arabic
فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا۟ فَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظًا ۖ إِنْ عَلَيْكَ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ۗ وَإِنَّآ إِذَآ أَذَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً فَرِحَ بِهَا ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَإِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ كَفُورٌ (٤٨)
```

```english
But if they disregard [your warnings], [keep in mind that] We have not sent you as their keeper. Your duty is only to communicate. Indeed when We let man taste Our mercy, he boasts about it; but should an ill visit him because of what his hands have sent ahead, then man is very ungrateful. (48)
```

```arabic
لِّلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ يَهَبُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ إِنَـٰثًا وَيَهَبُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ٱلذُّكُورَ (٤٩)
```

```english
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. He creates whatever He wishes; He gives females to whomever He wishes, and males to whomever He wishes, (49)
```

```arabic
أَوْ يُزَوِّجُهُمْ ذُكْرَانًا وَإِنَـٰثًا ۖ وَيَجْعَلُ مَن يَشَآءُ عَقِيمًا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ (٥٠)
```

```english
or He combines them males and females, and makes sterile whomever He wishes. Indeed, He is all-knowing, all-powerful. (50)
```

## (Commentary)

The opening of these verses is connected to the discourse on provision (الرزق) mentioned in His saying: **اللهُ لَطِيفٌ بِعِبادِهِ يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشاءُ** "Allah is all-attentive to His servants. He provides for whomever He wishes" (42:19). This was preceded by His saying: **لَهُ مَقالِيدُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَقْدِرُ** "To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth: He expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it" (42:12). It has been previously noted that among the forms of provision is the blessing of religion (الدين), which Allah, the Glorified, has granted to His faithful servants. Through this thematic link, the discourse on religion entered into the discussion on revelation (الوحي), for the explanation of which the verses of this Surah were sent down, turning back to it time and again.

Then, He mentions some signs of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — related to provision, such as the creation of the heavens and the earth, the dispersal of creatures within them, the ships sailing in the sea, and the granting of male and female children, or both, to whomever He wishes, while making whomever He wishes barren.

He then mentions that some of the provision He has given them is in this world, which is its fleeting enjoyment (متاع) that perishes with the world's end, while some is reserved for the believers in the Hereafter, which is better and more lasting. From here, the discourse transitions to the attributes of the believers and their fair end, and to the description of what the wrongdoers (الظالمون) — who are other than them — will encounter in their destiny of the terrors of the Resurrection and the punishment of the Hereafter.

Beyond that, interspersed within the verses, are summaries of certain rulings, warnings, intimidation, and the call to the Truth and the realities of divine knowledge (المعارف) in great abundance.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ بَسَطَ اللهُ الرِّزْقَ لِعِبادِهِ لَبَغَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلكِنْ يُنَزِّلُ بِقَدَرٍ ما يَشاءُ إِنَّهُ بِعِبادِهِ خَبِيرٌ بَصِيرٌ** "Spreade Allah His provision to [all] His servants, they would surely transgress on the earth. But He sends down in a [precise] measure whatever He wishes. Indeed, He is all-aware, all-seeing of His servants" (42:27). The term "tightening" (القدر) is the opposite of "expanding" (البسط); it means restriction (التضييق), and from it comes the previous saying: "He expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it (يَقْدِرُ)." The word *qadar* (القدر) — with a vowel on the *dal* or its absence — refers to the quantity (كمية) and geometry (هندسة) of a thing, as in His saying: "But He sends down in a [precise] measure (بِقَدَرٍ) whatever He wishes." Or it means making a thing according to a specific quantity, as in His saying: **فَقَدَرْنا فَنِعْمَ الْقادِرُونَ** "So We determined [it], and how well do We determine!" (77:23).

And transgression (البغي) is injustice (الظلم). His saying "of His servants" (بِعِبادِهِ) uses the explicit noun in place of the pronoun; the subtle point (النكتة) here is to indicate that He is all-aware and all-seeing of them because they are His servants, created by Him and sustained by Him, so they are not veiled from Him nor unknown to Him. Similarly, the previous "to His servants" (لِعِبادِهِ) is not devoid of an indication toward the granting of provision, for they are His servants, and the provision of the servant is the responsibility of his Master.

The meaning of the verse is: If Allah were to expand the provision for all His servants and satisfy everyone by His giving, they would surely commit injustice on the earth — because the nature of abundant wealth is insolence, vanity, arrogance, and tyranny, as the Exalted said: **إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَيَطْغى أَنْ رَآهُ اسْتَغْنى** "Indeed man becomes rebellious when he considers himself without need" (96:6-7). However, He sends down whatever provision He wishes in a precise measure (بِقَدَرٍ) and a specific quantity, for He is all-aware and all-seeing of His servants; He knows what each servant deserves and what suits him of wealth or poverty, and thus He grants him that.

In His saying: "But He sends down in a [precise] measure whatever He wishes," there is an explanation of the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — *the established way of Allah's action* — in granting provision with regard to the well-being of people's conditions. That is, the well-being of their condition has an effect on the determination of their provisions. This does not contradict what we observe of the tyranny of some wealthy individuals and the increase of their provision despite that, for there is another sunnah governing this one, which is the sunnah of trial (الابتلاء) and testing (الامتحان). The Exalted said: **إِنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ وَأَوْلادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ** "Your possessions and your children are only a test" (64:15). And another sunnah is that of deception (المكر) and gradual enticement (الاستدراج) — *the process by which a sinner is led step by step toward his ruin while being granted worldly blessings*. The Exalted said: **سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ وَأُمْلِي لَهُمْ إِنَّ كَيْدِي مَتِينٌ** "We will draw them on [to their ruin] from whence they know not. And I will grant them a respite; indeed My stratagem is profound" (7:182-183).

Thus, the sunnah of rectification through the measuring of provision is a primary sunnah (سنة ابتدائية) by which the human condition is rectified, unless Allah tests him, as He said: **وَلِيَبْتَلِيَ اللهُ ما فِي صُدُورِكُمْ وَلِيُمَحِّصَ ما فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ** "and so that Allah may test what is in your breasts, and that He may purge what is in your hearts" (3:154), or unless he changes the blessing and is ungrateful for it, in which case Allah changes His sunnah regarding him and gives him that which causes him to transgress. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ** "Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls" (13:11).

Just as the granting of wealth, children, and other outward blessings is part of the apportioned provision (الرزق المقسوم), so too are the true sciences (المعارف) and the heavenly laws (الشرائع) that culminate in revelation (الوحي) — in terms of their being sent down, the trial (الابتلاء) through them, and the engagement in acting upon them — part of the apportioned provision.

If the sciences and rulings were sent down in their entirety all at once — given their encompassment and comprehensiveness of all affairs of human life — it would have been difficult for people, and none would have believed in them except the rare individual (الأوحدي). However, Allah, the Glorified, sent them down to His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) gradually and with deliberation, thereby preparing the people by their acceptance of some to accept others. The Exalted said: **وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ** "A Quran which We have divided [into parts] so that you may read it to the people at intervals" (17:106).

Similarly, the higher sciences (المعارف العالية) that are within the depths of the simple religious sciences: if the veil were not drawn over them and they were explained to the general public in the same manner as the outward (الظواهر) meanings explained to them, they would not have been able to bear them, and their understandings would have rejected them, except for the rare individual. However, Allah, the Glorified, spoke to them regarding this in a manner of speaking from which everyone benefits according to the measure of his understanding and the expansion of his breast, as He said in a parable He struck for this: **أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَسالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِها** "He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity" (13:17).

Likewise, the legal rulings and obligations (التكاليف الشرعية): if all people were burdened with all of them, they would have been constrained by them and would not have endured them. But He, the Glorified, divided them among them according to the division of trials (الابتلاءات) that necessitate the direction of various obligations among them.

Thus, provision through sciences and laws, from whatever aspect it is considered, is like outward provision: it is allotted among people and measured according to the well-being of their condition.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي يُنَزِّلُ الْغَيْثَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما قَنَطُوا وَيَنْشُرُ رَحْمَتَهُ وَهُوَ الْوَلِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ** "It is He who sends down the rain after they have despaired, and unfolds His mercy; and He is the Guardian, the All-laudable" (42:28). Despair (القنوط) is hopelessness (اليأس), and *al-ghayth* (الغيث) is rain. It is said in *Majma' al-Bayan*: *Al-ghayth* is that which is beneficial in its time, whereas *al-matar* (المطر) may be beneficial or harmful, in its time or otherwise. End quote. The unfolding of mercy (نشر الرحمة) is the distribution of blessing among people by the growing of plants and the production of fruits caused by the rain.

In the verse, there is a transition from the discourse on provision to the signs of monotheism that have some connection to provisions, followed by verses in this same vein. The conclusion of the verse with the two names: the Guardian (الولي) and the All-laudable (الحميد) — which are among His Beautiful Names — is for praising Him for His beautiful act.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمِنْ آياتِهِ خَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَثَّ فِيهِما مِنْ دابَّةٍ** "Among His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth and whatever creatures He has scattered in them..." (42:29). Scattering (البث) is dispersal (التفريق); it is said: "the wind scattered (بث) the dust" when it stirs it up. A creature (الدابة) is anything that crawls on the earth, thus encompassing all animals. The meaning is clear.

The apparent meaning of the verse is that in the heavens there are creatures (دواب) just as there are on earth. The saying of some that the creatures in the heavens are the angels is refuted by the fact that applying the term *dabbah* (دواب) to angels is not customary.

His saying: **وَهُوَ عَلى جَمْعِهِمْ إِذا يَشاءُ قَدِيرٌ** "and He is able to gather them whenever He wishes" (42:29) is an indication of the gathering (حشر) of whatever creatures He has scattered in them. He expressed it with "gathering" (الجمع) as an opposite to "scattering" (البث), which is dispersal. There is no indication in His saying "gathering them" (جَمْعِهِمْ) — where He used the pronoun for rational beings (أولي العقل) — that the creatures in the heavens are rational like humans, because of His saying: **وَما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا طائِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَناحَيْهِ إِلَّا أُمَمٌ أَمْثالُكُمْ ما فَرَّطْنا فِي الْكِتابِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ** "There is no animal on land, nor a bird that flies with its wings, but they are communities like yourselves. We have not omitted anything from the Book. Then they will be mustered toward their Lord" (6:38).

And the All-Powerful (القدير) is among His Beautiful Names; it refers to Him in Whom power (القدرة) is centered and established. Al-Raghib said: Power (القدرة), when a human is described by it, is a name for a state (هيئة) by which he is able to perform a certain action. When Allah is described by it, it is the negation of incapacity (العجز) from Him. It is impossible for anyone other than Allah to be described with absolute power in meaning, even if the term is applied to him; rather, it is proper to say: "powerful over such-and-such." Whenever it is said "he is powerful," it is in a restricted sense. For this reason, no one other than Allah is described with power from one aspect without it being correct to describe him with incapacity from another, whereas Allah, the Exalted, is He from Whom incapacity is negated from every aspect.

The All-Powerful (القدير) is the Doer of what He wishes according to what wisdom (الحكمة) necessitates, neither exceeding it nor falling short of it; therefore, it is not correct to describe anyone with it except Allah. He said: "Indeed He is able (قدير) to do what He wishes." The All-Potent (المقتدر) is close to it, as in: **عِنْدَ مَلِيكٍ مُقْتَدِرٍ** "in the presence of an All-potent King" (54:55), but humans may be described by it. When used for Allah, its meaning is that of *al-Qadir*, and when used for humans, it means one who affects or acquires power. End quote.

This is good, except that his saying "power, when Allah is described by it, is the negation of incapacity from Him" contains an obvious laxity. For His essential attributes (صفاته الذاتية) — such as life, knowledge, and power — have positive meanings that are identical to the Essence (الذات), not negative meanings such that life means the absence of death, knowledge the absence of ignorance, and power the absence of incapacity, as the Sabians claim; the consequence of that would be the Essence being void of the attributes of perfection.

The truth is that the meaning of His power is His being such that He does what He wishes, and the consequence of this positive meaning is the negation of absolute incapacity from Him.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ** "Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]" (42:30). An affliction (المصيبة) is a calamity (النائبة) that strikes a person as if it were targeting him. What is meant by "what your hands have earned" are sins and evil deeds. His saying: "and He excuses many" means many of what your hands have earned, which are the evil deeds.

The address in the verse is social (اجتماعي), directed at the community as a whole, not broken down into individual addresses. Its consequence is that what is meant by the affliction that strikes them are general, encompassing calamities such as famine, high prices, epidemics, earthquakes, and so on.

Thus, the meaning is that the afflictions and calamities that strike your society and by which they are afflicted only strike you because of your sins, and Allah overlooks many of them and does not take account for them.

The verse is thus in the sense of His saying: **ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ** "Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned, that He may let them taste some of what they have done, so that they may come back" (30:41), and His saying: **وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلكِنْ كَذَّبُوا** "If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth. But they denied..." (7:96), and His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ** "Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls" (13:11), and other verses indicating that there is a specific connection between human actions and the cosmic order. If human society were to proceed according to what the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — necessitates of belief and action, goodness would descend upon it and blessings would be opened for it; but if they corrupt, He corrupts for them.

This is what this divine sunnah necessitates, unless it is superseded by the sunnah of trial or the sunnah of gradual enticement and respite, in which case the matter is reversed. The Exalted said: **ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنا مَكانَ السَّيِّئَةِ الْحَسَنَةَ حَتَّى عَفَوْا وَقالُوا قَدْ مَسَّ آباءَنَا الضَّرَّاءُ وَالسَّرَّاءُ فَأَخَذْناهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ** "Then We replaced the evil with good until they multiplied and said, ‘Hardship and ease had also visited our fathers.’ Then We seized them suddenly while they were unaware" (7:95).

It is also possible that the address in the verse is general, breaking down into addresses to individuals, such that whatever affliction strikes every person in his self, wealth, child, honor, or what pertains to him is based on a sin he committed and an evil deed he performed, and Allah excuses many of them.

In any case, the address in the verse is to all people, believer and disbeliever alike, which is what the context suggests and the following verse supports — this is first. What is meant by "what the hands have earned" are sins and evil deeds, rather than actions in general — this is second. And the afflictions that strike are merely the effects of actions in this world due to the connection and association between actions and them, rather than the [final] recompense for actions — this is third.

By what has been mentioned, the following is refuted: First, the objection raised against the generality of the verse regarding the afflictions that befell the Prophets (peace be upon them), who are infallible (معصومون) and have no sin, and the afflictions that befall children and the insane, who are not burdened with obligation (غير مكلفين) and thus have no sin; therefore, the verse must be specified (تخصيص) to exclude the afflictions of the Prophets, children, and the insane.

The way this is refuted is that the attribution of sin to them in His saying: "because of what your hands have earned" is evidence that the address in the verse is to those from whom the issuance of sin is possible; thus, it does not include the infallible and the non-obligated from the outset. So, the non-inclusion of the verse for them is by way of specialization (تخصص) — *the subject being outside the scope of the rule by its very nature* — rather than specification (تخصيص) — *the subject being intentionally excluded from a general rule*.

Second, what has been said: that the verse necessitates the forgiveness of all the sins of the believers, for they are either recompensed by the striking of afflictions or they are excused.

The way this is refuted is that the verse is intended to explain the connection of afflictions to sins and that sins have evil worldly effects, some of which strike a person without fail and some of which are excused and do not strike due to diverting causes and preventing wisdoms, such as maintaining family ties (صلة الرحم), charity (الصدقة), the prayer of a believer, repentance, and other things mentioned in the reports. As for the recompense of actions, the verse does not look toward it, as previously stated.

Furthermore, the address in the verse encompasses both the believer and the disbeliever, as previously indicated, and there is no sense in its being partial in its indication, such that it indicates forgiveness for the believer and its absence for the disbeliever.

After all this, the first view is the most appropriate.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا نَصِيرٍ** "You cannot frustrate [Him] on the earth, and you do not have any guardian or helper besides Allah" (42:31). The meaning of the verse is clear, and in its connection to what preceded, it conveys that you cannot frustrate Allah such that afflictions for your sins do not strike you, and you have no guardian besides Him to take charge of your affairs and ward off afflictions from you, nor any helper to assist you in warding them off.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمِنْ آياتِهِ الْجَوارِ فِي الْبَحْرِ كَالْأَعْلامِ** "Among His signs are the ships [sailing] in the sea like mountains" (42:32). *Al-jawari* (الجواري) is the plural of *jariyah*, which is the ship. *Al-a'lam* (الأعلام) is the plural of *'alam*, which is a mark (العلامة), and mountains are called by this name. The ships are likened to mountains due to their greatness and height. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ يَشأْ يُسْكِنِ الرِّيحَ فَيَظْلَلْنَ رَواكِدَ عَلى ظَهْرِهِ** "If He wishes, He calms the wind, whereat they remain motionless on its back..." (42:33). The pronoun in "He wishes" (يَشأْ) refers to Allah, the Exalted. *Zalla* (ظل) means "became." *Rawakid* (رواكِد) is the plural of *rakidah*, which is that which is stationary in its place. The meaning is: If Allah wishes, He stills the wind by which the ships sail, so they — the ships — become stationary on the surface of the sea.

And His saying: **إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ** "Indeed in that are signs for every patient and grateful [servant]" (42:33). The root of patience (الصبر) is restraint (الحبس), and the root of gratitude (الشكر) is the manifestation of the benefactor's blessing through word or deed. The meaning is: In what has been mentioned of the matter of the ships — their sailing on the surface of the sea due to the blowing of the winds, transporting people and their goods from coast to coast — are signs for everyone who restrains his soul from preoccupation with what does not concern him and occupies himself with reflecting on His blessings; and reflecting on the blessing is a form of gratitude.

It is said: What is meant by "every patient and grateful [servant]" is the believer, because the believer is never without either hardship or ease; if he is in hardship, he is among the patient, and if he is in ease, he is among the grateful.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَوْ يُوبِقْهُنَّ بِما كَسَبُوا وَيَعْفُ عَنْ كَثِيرٍ** "Or He wrecks them because of what they have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]" (42:34). *Al-ibaq* (الإيباق) is destruction (الإهلاك). The feminine pronoun refers to the ships, and the masculine pronoun refers to the people. "Wrecks them" (يوبقهن) and "excuses" (يعف) are conjoined to "calms" (يُسْكِنِ). The meaning is: If He wishes, He destroys the ships by drowning them because of the evil deeds they have earned, and He excuses many of them; that is, some of them are sufficient to necessitate destruction, even if He excuses many of them.

It is said: What is meant by their destruction is the destruction of their people, either metaphorically or by assuming a suppressed word. "Wrecks them" (يُوبِقْهُنَ), by conjunction to "calms" (يُسْكِنِ), is in the sense of: He sends the stormy winds and thus wrecks them. The meaning would be: If He wishes, He calms the wind... and if He wishes, He sends it and destroys them by drowning, while many of them are saved by [His] excuse. The summary: If He wishes, He calms the wind or sends it, thereby destroying people for their sins and saving people by excusing them. The affectation (التكلف) in this is not hidden.

It is also said: "excuses" (يَعْفُ) is conjoined to His saying: "calms the wind" up to His saying: "because of what they have earned," which is why it is conjoined with "and" (الواو) rather than "or" (أو). The meaning would be: If He wishes, He punishes them by calming [the wind] or by the storm, and if He wishes, He excuses many. This is as affected as the previous one.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَعْلَمَ الَّذِينَ يُجادِلُونَ فِي آياتِنا ما لَهُمْ مِنْ مَحِيصٍ** "and so that those who dispute Our signs may know that there is no escape for them" (42:35). It is said: This is a finality (غاية) conjoined to another suppressed one. The estimation is something like: so that He may manifest His power thereby, and so that those who dispute Our signs may know that they have no place of flight or salvation. This occurs frequently in the Noble Quran, except that the conjoined part in what has been reported is accompanied by the *lam* of finality, such as His saying: **وَلِيَعْلَمَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** "and so that Allah may know those who have faith" (3:140), and His saying: **وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ** "and so that he might be of those who possess certitude" (6:75).

Some have allowed it to be conjoined to the recompense of the condition (جزاء الشرط) by assuming "if" (إن), such as: "If you come to me, I will honor you and give (نصب) you such-and-such." The matter is a disputed grammatical issue; one should refer to what they have mentioned regarding it.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَما أُوتِيتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَمَتاعُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** "Whatever thing you have been given is only the enjoyment of the life of this world..." (42:36). This is a detailing of what was previously mentioned of provision and its division into what is with people of the provision of this world — which encompasses both the believer and the disbeliever — and what is with Allah of the provision of the Hereafter, which is exclusive to the believers. In it is a transition to mentioning the attributes of the believers and mentioning some of what the wrongdoers will encounter on the Day of Resurrection.

So His saying: "Whatever thing you have been given is only the enjoyment of the life of this world" — the address is to the people, as the context suggests, rather than the polytheists specifically. What is meant by "whatever thing you have been given" is all that has been granted to people and provided to them of bliss. The annexation of "enjoyment" (المتاع) to "life" (الحياة) is to indicate its cessation and its lack of stability and permanence. The meaning is: Every thing you have been given of what you possess is an enjoyment you enjoy for a few days.

And His saying: **وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقى لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ** "but what is with Allah is better and more lasting for those who have faith and put their trust in their Lord" (42:36). What is meant by "what is with Allah" is what Allah has stored as a reward to recompense the believers. The *lam* in "for those who have faith" (لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) denotes possession, and the adverbial phrase is redundant. It is said the *lam* is connected to His saying: "more lasting" (أَبْقى), but the first is more apparent. The fact that what is with Allah is "better" is because it is pure from pain and distress, and its being "more lasting" is because it is more permanent, with no end to its conclusion.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبائِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَالْفَواحِشَ وَإِذا ما غَضِبُوا هُمْ يَغْفِرُونَ** "[for] those who avoid major sins and indecencies, and forgive when they are angered" (42:37). This is conjoined to His saying: "for those who have faith." This verse and the two verses after it enumerate the good attributes of the believers. The saying of some that it is a new sentence (كلام مستأنف) is not supported by the context.

Major sins (كبائر الإثْمِ) are the great acts of disobedience that have immense evil effects; the Exalted has counted among them the drinking of wine and gambling, saying: **قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ** "Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them’" (2:219). Indecencies (الفواحش) is the plural of *fahishah*, which is the heinous and abominable sin; the Exalted has counted among them adultery and sodomy, saying: **وَلا تَقْرَبُوا الزِّنى إِنَّهُ كانَ فاحِشَةً** "Do not approach fornication. It is indeed an indecency" (17:32), and saying, narrating from Lot: **أَتَأْتُونَ الْفاحِشَةَ وَأَنْتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ** "What! Do you commit indecency while you see?" (27:54).

His saying: "avoid major sins and indecencies" — and this is in a Meccan Surah — is a summary indication of what will be detailed in the legislation of the prohibition of major sins and indecencies.

In His saying: "and forgive when they are angered," there is an indication of forgiveness at the time of anger, which is among the most specific attributes of the believers; therefore, He expressed it as He did and did not say: "and they forgive when they are angered." In the speech, there are aspects of emphasis, and it is not a restriction of forgiveness at the time of anger to them alone.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِرَبِّهِمْ وَأَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ** "[for] those who answer their Lord, and maintain the prayer..." (42:38). Answering (الاستجابة) is responding (الإجابة), and their answering their Lord is their responding to what He burdens them with of righteous deeds — as the context suggests. The mention of maintaining the prayer after it is of the type of mentioning the specific after the general due to its nobility.

Furthermore, it is apparent that the verses are Meccan, and at that time, things like Zakat, Khums, Fasting, and Jihad had not yet been legislated. In His saying: "those who answer their Lord," there is a summary indication of the legislated righteous deeds, similar to what preceded in His saying: "those who avoid..." etc., and a similar discourse applies to the following verses.

And His saying: **وَأَمْرُهُمْ شُورى بَيْنَهُمْ** "and their affairs are [settled] by counsel among themselves" (42:38). Al-Raghib said: *At-tashawur*, *al-mushawarah*, and *al-mashurah* mean extracting an opinion by referring some to others, from their saying: "I extracted (شرت) the honey" when you take it from its place and extract it. The Exalted said: "and consult them in the affairs." And *ash-shura* (الشورى) is the matter about which counsel is taken. The Exalted said: "and their affairs are [settled] by counsel among themselves." End quote. The meaning is: The matter they resolve upon is a counsel among them, they consult each other regarding it. It appears from some that it is a verbal noun (مصدر), and the meaning is: and their affair is consultation among themselves.

In any case, there is an indication in it that they are people of maturity (الرشد) and attainment of reality; they go to great lengths in extracting the correct opinion by referring to [various] intellects. Thus, the verse is close in meaning to the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ** "those who listen to the word [of Allah] and follow the best [interpretation] of it" (39:18).

And His saying: **وَمِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ يُنْفِقُونَ** "and who spend out of what We have provided them" (42:38) is an indication of spending wealth for the pleasure of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ إِذا أَصابَهُمُ الْبَغْيُ هُمْ يَنْتَصِرُونَ** "and those who, when an aggression visits them, help themselves" (42:39). Al-Raghib said: *Al-intisar* and *al-istinsar* mean seeking help. End quote. The meaning is: those who, when injustice strikes some of them, seek help from others; and since they are united upon the Truth as a single soul, it is as if the injustice struck all of them, so they sought resistance against it and prepared help for it.

It is narrated from some that *al-intisar* is in the sense of mutual helping (التناصر), similar to *ikhtasama* and *takhasama*, or *istabaqa* and *tasabaqa*. The meaning based on this is clear.

In any case, what is meant is their resistance to remove injustice; this does not contradict the forgiveness at the time of anger mentioned among their attributes, for resistance against injustice and closing its door upon society for whoever is able, and seeking help and mutual helping for its sake, are among the primordial obligations. The Exalted said: **وَإِنِ اسْتَنْصَرُوكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ فَعَلَيْكُمُ النَّصْرُ** "but if they seek your help in [the matter of] religion, then it is your duty to help [them]" (8:72), and He said: **فَقاتِلُوا الَّتِي تَبْغِي حَتَّى تَفِيءَ إِلى أَمْرِ اللهِ** "then fight the one that is rebellious until it reverts to Allah’s ordinance" (49:9).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَزاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِثْلُها** "The requital of evil is an evil like it..." (42:40) to the end of the verse is an explanation of what has been granted to the one who helps himself in his victory, which is that he may confront the aggressor with the like of his act, and this is not injustice or aggression.

It is said: The second one — which is what the one helping himself performs — is called an "evil" (سيئة) because it is in confrontation with the first, as the Exalted said: **فَمَنِ اعْتَدى عَلَيْكُمْ فَاعْتَدُوا عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا اعْتَدى عَلَيْكُمْ** "So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you" (2:194). Al-Zamakhshari said: Both acts — the first and its requital — are an "evil" because they grieve (تسوء) the one upon whom they descend. In this, there is a regard for the reality of the word's meaning and an indication that the requital of an evil with its like is only praised on the condition of similarity without excess.

And His saying: **فَمَنْ عَفا وَأَصْلَحَ فَأَجْرُهُ عَلَى اللهِ** "but whoever excuses and conciliates, his reward lies with Allah" (42:40) is a beautiful promise for forgiveness and conciliation. It is apparent that what is meant by conciliation (الإصلاح) is his rectifying his affair between himself and his Lord. It is also said: What is meant is his rectifying what is between him and his oppressor through forgiveness and overlooking.

And His saying: **إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الظَّالِمِينَ** "Indeed He does not like the wrongdoers" (42:40). It is said: In this is an explanation that He, the Exalted, did not encourage the oppressed to forgive the oppressor out of an inclination toward the oppressor or love for him, but rather so that the oppressed might thereby expose himself to the great reward, and because of His love for excellence and grace.

It is also said: What is meant is that He does not love the wrongdoer in retaliation or otherwise by his exceeding what is his to what is not his.

Although both views are good in themselves, the context of the verse does not support them, especially with His saying: "but whoever excuses and conciliates, his reward lies with Allah" intervening between the justification and the justified.

It is also possible that His saying: "Indeed He does not like the wrongdoers" is a justification for the very fact that the requital of an evil is an evil, without regard to similarity and equality.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَمَنِ انْتَصَرَ بَعْدَ ظُلْمِهِ فَأُولئِكَ ما عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ** "As for those who help themselves after being wronged, there is no way [to blame] them" (42:41) — up to His saying — **لَمِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ** "that is indeed a matter of resolution" (42:43). The pronoun in "his being wronged" (ظُلْمِهِ) refers to the oppressed. The annexation is of the verbal noun to its object.

The three verses are an explanation and a removal of ambiguity from His saying in the previous verse: "but whoever excuses and conciliates, his reward lies with Allah." For it is possible for the oppressed to imagine that in this is an annulment of his right to help himself. So He, the Glorified, explained first by His saying: "As for those who help themselves after being wronged, there is no way [to blame] them," that there is no way against the oppressed, nor any permit to invalidate their right in the divine law. The return of the singular pronoun to the relative pronoun first is in consideration of its wording, and the plural pronoun second is in consideration of its meaning.

He explained second by His saying: **إِنَّمَا السَّبِيلُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَظْلَمُونَ النَّاسَ وَيَبْغُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ** "The way [to blame] is only against those who wrong the people and commit aggression on the earth unduly" (42:42), that the entire "way" is against the wrongdoers in taking revenge upon them for the oppressed. He emphasized this at the end by His saying: **أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ** "For such there is a painful punishment."

And He explained third by His saying: **وَلَمَنْ صَبَرَ وَغَفَرَ إِنَّ ذلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ** "As for those who are patient and forgive, that is indeed a matter of resolution" (42:43), that the call to patience and forgiveness is not an annulment of the right to help oneself, but rather an instruction toward a virtue that is among the greatest of virtues; for in forgiveness is patience, which is among the matters of resolution. He emphasized the speech with the *lam* of the oath first, and with the *lam* in the predicate of *inna* second, to convey the importance of its content.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ** "Whomever Allah leads astray has no guardian after Him..." (42:44) etc. When He mentioned the believers with their attributes and that they have with Allah their provision stored for them — in which is the happiness of their destiny to which Allah guided them — He turned to others, namely the wrongdoers who are despairing of that guidance leading to happiness and deprived of this noble provision. He explained that Allah, the Glorified, led them astray due to their disbelief and denial, so they do not reach the provision that is with Him, nor does He make them happy with it. They have no guardian besides Him to take charge of their affairs and provide them with what Allah has deprived them of. Thus, they are empty-handed, wishing upon witnessing the punishment to return to the world to perform righteous deeds so they might be like the believers.

So His saying: "Whomever Allah leads astray..." etc., is of the type of placing the cause — which is Allah's leading them astray and the absence of another guardian to take charge of their affairs, guide them, and provide for them — in place of the effect, which is guidance and provision.

And His saying: **وَتَرَى الظَّالِمِينَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا الْعَذابَ يَقُولُونَ هَلْ إِلى مَرَدٍّ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ** "You will see the wrongdoers, when they sight the punishment, saying, ‘Is there any way to return?’" (42:44) is an indication of their wishing to return to the world after despairing of happiness and witnessing the punishment.

And "you see" (تَرَى) is a general address directed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) insofar as he is a seer, and its meaning is: you see, and everyone who is a seer sees. In it is an indication that they wish for that in the presence of all witnesses. *Al-maradd* (المرد) is the return (الرد).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَتَراهُمْ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْها خاشِعِينَ مِنَ الذُّلِّ يَنْظُرُونَ مِنْ طَرْفٍ خَفِيٍ** "You will see them being exposed to it, humbled by abasement, looking with a furtive glance..." (42:45). The pronoun in "to it" (عَلَيْها) refers to the Fire, as indicated by the context. A "furtive glance" (طرف خفي) is a weak one; one only looks with a furtive glance at terrifying horrors when one is afflicted by them, for he does not want to turn away and thus be heedless of them, nor does he dare to fill his sight with them, like one who is being watched looking at the sword. The rest is clear.

And His saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّ الْخاسرِينَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَأَهْلِيهِمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ** "The faithful will say, ‘The [real] losers are those who have lost themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection’" (42:45). That is, the losers in every sense and in reality are those who lost themselves by depriving them of salvation, and their families by not benefiting from them on the Day of Resurrection. It is said their families are their spouses from the *huris* and their servants in Paradise had they believed; this is not without merit considering the verses on the inheritance of Paradise.

This saying attributed to the believers is only said by them on the Day of Resurrection — the expression in the past tense is due to the certainty of its occurrence — not in this world as appears from some. For there is no reason for His attributing the saying of the believers in this world in such a context. Nor are the speakers all the believers, whoever they may be; rather, they are the perfect ones among them who are permitted to speak, those who speak only the pure truth, such as the People of the Heights (أصحاب الأعراف) and the witnesses of actions. The Exalted said: **يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ** "The day it comes, no soul shall speak except by His leave" (11:105). And He said: **لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ وَقالَ صَواباً** "they will not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted and who says what is right" (78:38).

Therefore, no heed should be paid to what has been said: that the mentioned saying was only attributed to the believers to indicate their rejoicing in what they were provided that day of honor and their salvation from loss; otherwise, the saying is the saying of everyone from whom speech is possible among the people of the gathering, just as the vision mentioned before it is the vision of everyone from whom vision is possible.

And His saying: **أَلا إِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ فِي عَذابٍ مُقِيمٍ** "Look! The wrongdoers are indeed in a lasting punishment" (42:45) is a recording against them of the punishment and that it is permanent, never-ending. It is allowed that it be from the completion of the believers' speech.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما كانَ لَهُمْ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ يَنْصُرُونَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** "They will have no guardians to help them besides Allah..." (42:46). This expression — I mean His saying: "They will have no..." etc., rather than saying: "They have no guardian" as was said first — is to indicate the manifestation of the falsehood of their claim to the guardianship of their guardians in the world, and that it was false from the very beginning.

And His saying: **وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ** "and whomever Allah leads astray, there is no way for him" (42:46) is suitable as a justification for the beginning of the verse, and it is like a conclusion for all that preceded of the discourse on the state of the wrongdoers in their destiny. It is a type of turning back to what preceded of the discourse on the legislation of the law and the way (السبيل) through revelation.

It is an allusion to the fact that there is no way to happiness except the way of Allah which He legislated for His servants through the path of revelation and messengership. So whomever He leads astray from His way due to his disbelief and denial of His way, there is no way for him by which he may be guided to the happiness of the destiny and escape from punishment and destruction.

His saying, the Exalted: **اسْتَجِيبُوا لِرَبِّكُمْ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا مَرَدَّ لَهُ مِنَ اللهِ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ مَلْجَإٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَما لَكُمْ مِنْ نَكِيرٍ** "Answer your Lord before there comes a day for which there is no turning back from Allah. You will have no refuge on that day, nor will you have [any way of] denial" (42:47). This is a call and a warning of the Day of Resurrection mentioned in the previous verses, as the context provides. The saying of some that what is meant by the "day" is the day of death is not well-founded.

In His saying: "for which there is no turning back from Allah" (لا مَرَدَّ لَهُ مِنَ اللهِ), the *la* is for the negation of the genus, *maradd* is its noun, *lahu* is its predicate, and *min Allah* is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for *maradd*. The meaning is: a day for which there is no turning back from the side of Allah; that is, it is a decreed, inevitable matter that Allah will never turn back. It is thus in the sense of what is repeated in His speech of describing the Day of Resurrection as being that in which there is no doubt.

They have mentioned other aspects of parsing for the sentence — I mean His saying: "a day for which there is no turning back from Allah" — but there is no benefit in reporting them.

And His saying: "You will have no refuge on that day, nor will you have [any way of] denial." A refuge (الملجأ) is a sanctuary to which one resorts. *An-nakir* (النكير) — as has been said — is a verbal noun in the sense of denial (الإنكار). The meaning is: You will have no sanctuary to which you may resort from Allah, nor will you have any denial of what issued from you, due to the manifestation of the matter from every side.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا فَما أَرْسَلْناكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظاً إِنْ عَلَيْكَ إِلَّا الْبلاغُ** "But if they withdraw, We did not send you to be their keeper; your duty is only to deliver [the message]..." (42:48). This is a turning from addressing them to addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to inform that what he has been burdened with of the matter is only the delivery, nothing more. He was sent as a deliverer of the religion of Allah; his duty is only the delivery. He was not sent as a keeper over them, responsible for their faith and obedience, such that he should prevent them from withdrawing and exhaust himself for their turning toward it.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنَّا إِذا أَذَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً فَرِحَ بِها وَإِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَإِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ كَفُورٌ** "Indeed when We let man taste a mercy from Us, he exults in it; but if an ill visits them because of what their hands have sent ahead, then man is indeed very ungrateful" (42:48). Exulting (الفرح) in mercy is an allusion to being preoccupied with the blessing and forgetting the Benefactor. What is meant by the "ill" (السيئة) is the affliction that grieves a person when it strikes him. His saying: "then man is indeed very ungrateful" (فَإِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ كَفُورٌ) uses the explicit noun in place of the pronoun; the subtle point in it is to record blame and reproach upon him by mentioning him by his name.

In the verse, there is a sense of their withdrawal and a rebuke of them under the title of "man" who is preoccupied with the world; for by his nature, he is an ally of heedlessness. If he is reminded by a blessing he is given, the exulting in it diverts him from the remembrance of Allah; and if he is reminded by an ill that strikes him because of what his hands have sent ahead, ungratefulness (الكفران) occupies him from the remembrance of his Lord. Thus, he is in heedlessness of the remembrance of his Lord whether in blessing or in misfortune, such that almost no call succeeds with him and no exhortation benefits him.

His saying, the Exalted: **لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ** "To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. He creates whatever He wishes..." (42:49) to the end of the two verses. The two verses have a type of connection to what preceded of the discourse on provision, since the children mentioned in them are of the type of provision.

It is said: They are connected to the previous verse where the tasting of mercy and the striking of an ill were mentioned, and that man exults in mercy and is ungrateful in the ill. So He, the Exalted, mentioned in these two verses that the kingdom of the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah, the Glorified; He creates whatever He wishes. Thus, it is not for the one who tastes His mercy to exult in it and be preoccupied with it, nor for the one whom an ill strikes to be ungrateful and object. Rather, to Him belongs the creation and the command; so the one shown mercy must be grateful, and the one afflicted must return to Him.

This is made unlikely by the fact that He, the Exalted, did not attribute the ill in the previous verse to Himself, but rather to what their hands sent ahead; so it does not suit the attribution of both types in this verse to His wish and the call to submit to it.

In any case, His saying: "To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. He creates whatever He wishes" contains a restriction of kingdom and sovereignty to Him alone over the entire world, and that creation is contingent upon His wish without there being any matter that necessitates the wish upon Him or compels Him to create.

And His saying: **يَهَبُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ إِناثاً وَيَهَبُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ الذُّكُورَ** "He gives females to whomever He wishes, and gives males to whomever He wishes" (42:49). *Al-inath* (الإناث) is the plural of *untha* (female), and *adh-dhukur* (الذكور) and *adh-dhikran* (الذكران) are plurals of *dhakar* (male). The apparent meaning of the contrast is that what is meant is the giving of females only to whomever He wishes, and the giving of males only to whomever He wishes; therefore, the wish was repeated. It is said: The reason for the definiteness of "the males" (الذكور) is that they are the ones sought by them and present in their minds, especially the Arabs.

And His saying: **أَوْ يُزَوِّجُهُمْ ذُكْراناً وَإِناثاً** "or He joins them, both males and females..." (42:50). That is, He gathers between them such that they are both males and females together; for *al-tazwij* (التزويج) in the language is gathering. And His saying: **وَيَجْعَلُ مَنْ يَشاءُ عَقِيماً** "and He makes whomever He wishes barren" (42:50). That is, she does not give birth and no child is born to him. Since this is also a category in its own right, He restricted it with the wish like the first two categories. As for the category of gathering between males and females, it is in reality a gathering between the first two categories, so He sufficed with what was mentioned of the wish in them.

And His saying: **إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ** "Indeed He is all-knowing, all-powerful" (42:50) is a justification for what preceded; that is, He is all-knowing, He does not increase what He increases out of ignorance, and all-powerful, He does not decrease what He decreases out of incapacity.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Hakim narrated and authenticated it, and al-Bayhaqi from Ali, who said: "This verse was only sent down regarding the People of the Bench (أصحاب الصفة): **وَلَوْ بَسَطَ اللهُ الرِّزْقَ لِعِبادِهِ لَبَغَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ** 'Spreade Allah His provision to [all] His servants, they would surely transgress on the earth.' That was because they said: 'If only we had...' and they wished for the world."

I say: Based on this, the verse would be Medinan, but the narration is more like an application (تطبيق) than an occasion of revelation (شأن النزول).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: **وَلَوْ بَسَطَ اللهُ الرِّزْقَ لِعِبادِهِ لَبَغَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ**, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "If He had done so, they would have done so — but He made them in need of one another — and He made them servants thereby. If He had made them wealthy, they would have transgressed. **وَلكِنْ يُنَزِّلُ بِقَدَرٍ ما يَشاءُ** 'But He sends down in a [precise] measure whatever He wishes' of what He knows will rectify them in their religion and their world. **إِنَّهُ بِعِبادِهِ خَبِيرٌ بَصِيرٌ** 'Indeed, He is all-aware, all-seeing of His servants.'"

In *al-Majma'*, Anas narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from Gabriel from Allah, majestic is His mention: "Indeed, among My servants is he whom nothing rectifies except illness; if I were to make him healthy, it would corrupt him. And indeed, among My servants is he whom nothing rectifies except health; if I were to make him ill, it would corrupt him. And indeed, among My servants is he whom nothing rectifies except wealth; if I were to make him poor, it would corrupt him. And indeed, among My servants is he whom nothing rectifies except poverty; if I were to make him wealthy, it would corrupt him. That is because I manage My servants by My knowledge of their hearts."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, my father narrated to me from Ibn Abi 'Umayr from Mansur bin Yunus from Abu Hamzah from al-Asbagh bin Nubatah from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), who said: "I heard him saying: 'I shall narrate to you a narration that every Muslim ought to take to heart.' Then he turned to us and said: 'Allah does not punish a believing servant in this world except that Allah is more Wise, more Generous, and more Glorious than to return to his punishment on the Day of Resurrection.'"

Then he said: "And Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, may test the believer with an affliction in his body, his wealth, his child, or his family." Then he recited this verse: **وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ** "Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]," and he gestured with his hand three times.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Hisham bin Salim from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Behold, there is no vein that throbs, nor a calamity, nor a headache, nor an illness except due to a sin — and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, in His Book: **وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ**." He said: Then he said: "And what Allah excuses is more than what He takes to account."

I say: This meaning was narrated through another path from Misma' from him (peace be upon him). A similar report was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Hasan from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and its wording is: "When this verse was sent down: **وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ**, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'By the One in Whose hand is my soul — there is no scratch of a wood, nor throbbing of a vein, nor calamity of a stone, nor stumble of a foot except due to a sin; and what Allah excuses from it is more.'"

In *al-Kafi* also, with its chain of narration from Ali bin Ri'ab, who said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ**. Have you considered what befell Ali and his household (peace be upon them) after him — is it because of what their hands earned, while they are the People of the House of purity, infallible (معصومون)?"

He replied: "Indeed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to repent to Allah and seek forgiveness every day and night a hundred times without any sin. Indeed, Allah singles out His friends (أولياءه) for afflictions so that He may reward them for them."

In *al-Majma'*, it is narrated from Ali (peace be upon him) that he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The best verse in the Book of Allah is this verse. O Ali, there is no scratch of a wood nor calamity of a foot except due to a sin; and what Allah has excused in the world, He is more Generous than to return to it, and what He has punished for in the world, He is more Just than to repeat it upon His servant.'"

I say: It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from several of the masters of the collections from Ali (peace be upon him) from him (ص). The gist of the narration is that His saying, the Exalted: **وَما أَصابَكُمْ** — the verse — is specific to the believers and the address is to them, and its import is the forgiveness of all their sins, so they are not punished for them in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — nor on the Day of Resurrection. For the verse restricts sins into those taken to account by the striking of an affliction and those excused. The import of the narration is the negation of taking to account after taking to account, and the negation of taking to account after excuse.

However, the matter is problematic: First, from the aspect of what you have known that the verse is in a context that conveys the generality of the address to the believer and the disbeliever.

Second, from the aspect of the narration's contradiction to what has been reported in numerous reports — perhaps reaching the level of thematic mass-transmission (التواتر المعنوي) — that some believers will be punished in their graves or in the Hereafter.

Third, from the aspect of the narration's opposition to the outward meanings of the verses indicating that the place of recompense for actions is the Abode of the Hereafter, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ يُؤاخِذُ اللهُ النَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِمْ ما تَرَكَ عَلَيْها مِنْ دَابَّةٍ وَلكِنْ يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَلا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ** "Were Allah to take the people to task for their wrongdoing, He would not leave any living being upon it. But He respites them until a specified time; so when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it" (16:61), and other verses indicating that every injustice and sin is taken to account, and that the place of taking to account is after death and in the Resurrection, except for what is forgiven by repentance, or removed by a good deed, or by intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — in the Hereafter, or the like.

Furthermore, the verse — I mean His saying: **وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ** — as previously indicated, is not apparent in the striking of the affliction being a recompense for the action, nor in the excuse being in the sense of annulling the recompense. Rather, it is the worldly effect of the evil deed that strikes once and is erased another.

Thus, it is appropriate that the narration — if accepted — be interpreted as taking a good opinion of Allah, the Glorified.

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَمْرُهُمْ شُورى بَيْنَهُمْ**, it has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said: "No man consults anyone except that he is guided to maturity (الرشد)."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **يَهَبُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ إِناثاً**, meaning there are no males with them; **وَيَهَبُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ الذُّكُورَ**, meaning there are no females with them; **أَوْ يُزَوِّجُهُمْ ذُكْراناً وَإِناثاً**, meaning He gives to whomever He wishes both males and females together — He gathers for him sons and daughters; that is, He gives them all to one person.

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain of narration from al-Husayn bin 'Alwan from Zayd bin Ali from his fathers from Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "A man came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, indeed my father has proceeded to a slave of mine and emancipated him in a manner intended to harm me.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'You and your wealth are from the gift of Allah to your father — you are an arrow from his quiver. **يَهَبُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ إِناثاً وَيَهَبُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ الذُّكُورَ أَوْ يُزَوِّجُهُمْ ذُكْراناً وَإِناثاً وَيَجْعَلُ مَنْ يَشاءُ عَقِيماً**. Your father's emancipation is valid. Your father may take from your wealth and your body — but it is not for you to take from his wealth nor from his body anything except by his permission.'"

I say: This meaning is narrated from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) in the answer to the questions of Muhammad bin Sinan in *al-'Ilal*, and it is narrated through the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah from Aisha from him (ص).
